title: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism
wikitext:
[]: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by SAWgunner » Mon Jun 27, 2011 10:37 pm Ever since I first began to really understand the Human Instrumentality Project, I was struck by the similarities between it and the various totalitarian ideologies inflicted on mankind throughout history. Whether it's Naziism, Communism, or the radical democratic ideals of the French Revolution, all totalitarianisms have shared a utopian dream for the perfection of the human race. In the case of Instrumentality, this grand scheme is the elimination of human loneliness rather than the elimination of inferior human beings or of inequality which, in the minds of those movements' intellectuals, were the great stumbling block to perfection. As was the case with real-world totalitarianism's champions, the proponents of Instrumentality seemed to talk a good game about elevating mankind to godliness and whatnot. After all, people who would go through so much trouble to seek such a wonderful goal as the end of human suffering must really care a great deal about their fellow man. However, just like the Bolsheviks who suppressed labor unions in Russia and who shot, deported, and incarcerated millions of those they were ostensibly trying to help, Seele's means to achieve these ends were quite monstrous. They nearly destroyed the Earth, killed billions of people, rendered the human race down to a comingling sludge of souls, manipulated world governments, suppressed the truth of all these atrocities, and destroyed anyone who stood in the way of their mad dream. All for the purpose of mankind's betterment. An important distinction between totalitarianism and authoritarianism in Political Science is made in the degree of control exerted by the state and the ends for those means. In authoritarian regimes, the political activities of the individual are suppressed for the purpose of protecting the state from threats to its rule. Citizens are, to varying degrees, free to do what they please so long as their activities don't impinge on the state's prerogatives. Totalitarian regimes don't just care about the individual's politics, they care about every aspect of his existence. In order to remake the world to his desires, the totalitarian wants his subject to eat People's Democratic cereal in the morning, whistle People's Democratic songs on his way to work at the People's Democratic factory, unwind by playing People's Democratic ping-pong, and dream People's Democratic dreams while they sleep. The totalitarian concerns himself with every aspect of his subjects' lives, down to their very thoughts. Likewise, those who strove for Instrumentality seek to yoke humans' very souls to their dream. What greater degree of control can one have over another than his soul? Not only that, but the very purpose of Instrumentality is the subjugation of the individual to a larger whole; a totalitarian dream. At the risk of speaking outside my competence, modern totalitarianism and Evangelion (thus also Instrumentality) derive from similar roots. Evangelion was inspired by the experience of one man, Anno, with the alienation and degeneration which seems endemic to developed Western societies today (since Japan has adopted many of the same ideas and institutions as more traditionally "Western" cultures, I think it should be included). Likewise, totalitarianism was inspired by alienation caused by the cataclysmic loss of faith in the traditional Western mold in the face of industrialization and the First World War. Now don't get me wrong, I don't think for a second that Evangelion is some sort of commentary on totalitarianism. It's just that the similarities are glaring to me. Though I wouldn't be surprised if Anno had drawn some inspiration from those ideas and their historical aftermath that one picks up just by interacting with the world. Your thoughts?

[]: Re: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by symbv » Mon Jun 27, 2011 10:44 pm SAWgunner wrote: Now don't get me wrong, I don't think for a second that Evangelion is some sort of commentary on totalitarianism.

[]:             Quote Post by katastroff » Mon Jun 27, 2011 11:01 pm I thought HIP was more transhumanism, or some religious-technocratic reconciliation by the head-honchos of SEELE. Nerv and SEELE seem to completely dominate the political globe, but it doesn't seem like they control all aspects of civilian life like totalitarian regimes do.

[]:             Quote Post by Mayo Thompson » Tue Jun 28, 2011 12:17 am I'd go with it being transhumanism as well. I don't see 3I as being a plan to destroy exploitation, collectivize humanity, increase efficiency, or dominate; but to destroy all non-conscious aspects of being, nobody wins, nobody loses, nobody rules. SEELE was definitely some kind of Plutocratic shadow-government before 3I though.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Jun 28, 2011 12:26 am Instrumentality is more the OPPOSITE of Transhumanism, of anything. It's all about breaking down the human condition rather than building it up.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 28, 2011 2:35 am Transhumanism isn't the opposite of totalitarianism. Some facets of each can go hand in hand in if the state demands better citizens.

[]:             Quote Post by SAWgunner » Tue Jun 28, 2011 12:39 pm Of course the mechanisms of control are different, Seele doesn't have camps or an omnipresent secret police after all. But the ends match those of real-world totalitarianism in their utopian aspect. Mayo Thompson wrote: I'd go with it being transhumanism as well. I don't see 3I as being a plan to destroy exploitation, collectivize humanity, increase efficiency, or dominate; but to destroy all non-conscious aspects of being, nobody wins, nobody loses, nobody rules. SEELE was definitely some kind of Plutocratic shadow-government before 3I though.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Tue Jun 28, 2011 3:50 pm SAWgunner wrote: But the ends match those of real-world totalitarianism in their utopian aspect.

[]: Re: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by CJD » Tue Jun 28, 2011 6:11 pm SAWgunner wrote: <Blip> Now don't get me wrong, I don't think for a second that Evangelion is some sort of commentary on totalitarianism. It's just that the similarities are glaring to me. Though I wouldn't be surprised if Anno had drawn some inspiration from those ideas and their historical aftermath that one picks up just by interacting with the world. Your thoughts?

[]: Re: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by SAWgunner » Tue Jun 28, 2011 8:59 pm CJD wrote: Totalitarianism would have more in line with the Borg or something of their like, the destruction of free will. Like I said in the quote, I never took instrumentality to be the death of the individual, just the death of individuality. I acknowledge that it's kind of weird, but I hope my intent is clear when I say that.

[]: Re: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by CJD » Tue Jun 28, 2011 11:14 pm SAWgunner wrote: You're forgetting the utopian aspects of totalitarian thought. The death of pain is exactly the sort of thing that intellectual dreamers would fill concentration camps for.

[]: Re: Instrumentality and Totalitarianism             Quote Post by katastroff » Fri Jul 01, 2011 4:54 pm CJD wrote: I might regret this analogy, but think of it like a company merger. You merge Company X and Company Y, and rename it Company Z. Company X, the people that were a part of it, they still have jobs. Company Y, the people that were part of it, they still have jobs. Both companies continue to make the same products, the same guidelines, but instead its under a new name, with new headquarters, new stocks, a new identity. Company X and Y still exist, but they aren't individual companies anymore, they're now combined to be Company Z.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jul 01, 2011 5:18 pm Some forms of transhumanism actoally spring from the wilder shores of Russian Orrthodox thought.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Jul 04, 2011 4:41 pm I did drew once a parallel between Seele's ideology and fascism and communism.

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Tue Jul 05, 2011 12:56 am RE: SAWgunner and "mankind's benefit" I suppose you could say that Instrumentality is somewhat fascistic in that they're forcing their "solution" upon humanity. They have literally no faith in humanity or people...I brought up on GB.net the idea that SEELE and NERV are probably direct descendants of the Cult of Gozer, which believed that society would not survive for very long and so set about hastening the end of the world through various arcane/mystical means (the Dead Sea Scrolls, Shandor's rituals, etc.) Except, what if everyone *doesn't* want the world to end...? Back to EVA, though: I think there's a distinct parallel between Shinji and SEELE...one can almost imagine a bunch of socially-awkward, like-minded former child prodigies in various fields kind of finally finding someone on the same wavelength after spending their whole lives being shat upon by everyone they know. They'd think, "We suffered a great deal, and we'd rather not see anyone else suffer...we'd rather not suffer anymore either." And yet their AT fields are so strong that they don't consider the bitter irony of their plans: namely, that they're about to cause much more short-term suffering than solve.

[]:             Quote Post by SAWgunner » Tue Jul 05, 2011 2:04 am ^Preaching the good word!

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Tue Jul 05, 2011 9:37 am SAWgunner wrote: ^Preaching the good word!

[]:             Quote Post by SAWgunner » Tue Jul 05, 2011 12:59 pm The similarities to Instrumentality and Totalitarianism. EDIT, clarification: I'm merely agreeing with you.

Whether it's Naziism, Communism, or the radical democratic ideals of the French Revolution, all totalitarianisms have shared a uto
